viewing-file-contents.label: Viewing File Contents
viewing-file-contents.summary: Read text files with cat, preview long files with head and tail, and count lines with wc -l.

viewing-file-contents-try-it-1.title: Read a short note
viewing-file-contents-try-it-1.prompt: The event lead left a short agenda. Use `cat agenda.txt` to read it.
viewing-file-contents-try-it-1.hint: Use the terminal. Check your work with `pwd`, `ls`, `cat`, or `find` before clicking Run.

help.concept: This task checks the workspace you build, not one perfect command line.
help.hint_1: Create folders before putting files inside them.
help.hint_2: When you are unsure, inspect the folder first, then make the next small change.

viewing-file-contents-try-it-2.hint: Use the terminal. Check your work with `pwd`, `ls`, `cat`, or `find` before clicking Run.

cat-whole-file.title: Use `cat` for a small file
cat-whole-file.bodyMarkdown:
`cat` prints the whole file. It is best for short notes.

```bash
cat agenda.txt
```

If the file is long, the output may be too much to read comfortably.

head-tail-preview.title: Use `head` and `tail` for previews
head-tail-preview.bodyMarkdown:
`head` shows the beginning of a file. `tail` shows the end.

```bash
head event-log.txt
tail event-log.txt
```

These are helpful when a file has many lines and you only need a quick check.

wc-line-count.title: Use `wc -l` to count lines
wc-line-count.bodyMarkdown:
`wc -l` counts lines in a file.

```bash
wc -l guest-list.txt
```

For checklists, logs, and lists of names, line count is a fast way to confirm that the file is roughly the size you expect.
